utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Hi Dmytro! How are you doing today?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Hello Sarah! I am good, thank you. And you?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: I'm great, thanks for asking! So how was your week? Busy at work?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Yes, very busy. I am working on new project. Big project for... how to say... bank.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Oh interesting! You're working on a new project for a bank. What kind of project is it?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Is... application. Mobile application. I am developer, so I write code for this.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: That sounds really cool! You write code for mobile applications. And do you enjoy it?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Yes, I like very much. But sometimes is difficult because client want many things and... time is not enough.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: I understand! The client wants many things and there isn't enough time. That's very common in IT, right?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Yes, exactly! Always like this. But okay, I like my job.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: That's great that you enjoy it. Now Dmytro, today we're going to talk about something very useful - your daily routine and telling the time. Does that sound good?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Oh yes, this is good topic. I need this for work because I have meetings with... people from other countries and I must to say time correct.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: That's a great reason to learn! And notice - we say 'I must say' or 'I have to say', not 'I must to say'. 'Must' doesn't need 'to' after it.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Ah, okay. I must say. Without 'to'. Got it.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Perfect! So before we start with the time, tell me - what time did you wake up today?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Today I wake up on seven o'clock.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Good try! So we say 'I woke up' because it was this morning - in the past. And we say 'at seven o'clock', not 'on'. Can you try again?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: I... woke up at seven o'clock.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Excellent! And what about yesterday? What time did you wake up yesterday?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Yesterday I wake up late. Maybe... eight? Yes, eight o'clock.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Remember, yesterday is past tense - 'I woke up late'. But don't worry, we'll practise that more. And is seven o'clock early for you?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: For me, seven is normal. My wife wake up at six because she go to... she is teacher, she go to school early.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Oh, your wife is a teacher! That's nice. And notice - 'my wife wakes up', 'she goes to school'. When we talk about he, she, or it, we add an 's' to the verb. She wakes up, she goes.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Ah yes, the 's'. She wakes up. She goes to school. I always forget this.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: It takes time! Don't worry. Okay, so today we're going to talk about the time and daily routine, and we'll practise that 's' a lot. Ready?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Yes, ready!

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Great! So Dmytro, you already know 'o'clock', right? Seven o'clock, eight o'clock. Now let's learn some more ways to say the time.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: So when the minutes are thirty, we can say 'half past'. For example, seven thirty is 'half past seven'. Can you say that?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Half past seven. Like... половина на сьому... half past seven.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Exactly! And what about eight thirty?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Half past eight.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Perfect! Now, when the minutes are fifteen, we say 'quarter past'. So seven fifteen is 'quarter past seven'. Try it!

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Quarter past seven. Quarter is like... чверть?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Yes! Like чверть, a quarter - one fourth. And six fifteen?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Quarter past six.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Brilliant! Now, there's also 'quarter to'. This means fifteen minutes before the hour. So six forty-five is 'quarter to seven'. Because it's fifteen minutes before seven.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Hmm, quarter to seven. So forty-five is quarter to... next hour?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Exactly right! Forty-five minutes is quarter to the next hour. So what's eight forty-five?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Quarter to... nine!

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Well done! Let me check. What about eleven thirty?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Half past eleven.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: And three fifteen?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Quarter past three.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: And five forty-five?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Quarter to... six.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Excellent! You're catching on quickly! Now, we also use 'past' and 'to' for other minutes. Ten past seven means seven ten. Twenty to eight means seven forty. Does that make sense?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: So... past is after, and to is before?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Exactly! 'Past' means after the hour, 'to' means before the next hour. So how would you say seven twenty?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Twenty past seven.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: And seven fifty?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Hmm... ten to... eight?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Yes! Seven fifty is ten to eight. You understand the system. Wonderful!

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Is like Ukrainian, similar system. But we use... different words.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Good observation! Many languages have similar ways to express time. Now, one more thing - in everyday English, many people also just say the numbers. Like 'seven twenty' instead of 'twenty past seven'. Both are correct.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Oh, this is easier! Seven twenty, seven fifty. Like in Ukrainian we also can say just numbers.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Exactly! Now let me ask you some questions. What time do you usually start work?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: I start work on nine o'clock. But sometimes nine thirty because... traffic.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Good! Remember - 'at nine o'clock', not 'on'. We use 'at' for times and 'on' for days. At nine o'clock, on Monday.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: At nine o'clock. Okay. At. I always say 'on' because in Ukrainian we say 'о дев'ятій', and 'о' sounds like 'on'.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: That's a really good explanation of why you make that mistake! Being aware of it will help you remember. So say it again - what time do you start work?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: I start work at nine o'clock.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: And what time do you finish?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: I finish at... six. But sometimes later because project have deadline.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: The project has a deadline. Remember the 's' - the project has. And we say 'the project' with an article. Can you try the full sentence?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: I finish at six but sometimes later because the project has... deadline.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: 'Has a deadline' - we need the article 'a' before deadline. The project has a deadline.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: The project has a deadline. Okay, articles is very hard for me.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Articles are hard for most Ukrainian speakers! Don't worry, we'll keep practising. Now, what time do you usually have lunch at work?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: I have lunch at one o'clock. Sometimes half past twelve.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Great use of 'half past'! And where do you eat? At the office?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Yes, in office. We have kitchen in office. I bring food from home because my wife cook very good.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: That's lovely! A couple of things - 'in the office' and 'at the office' both work, but we need 'the'. And 'my wife cooks very well'. Remember the 's' for she, and 'well' not 'good' when we describe how someone does something.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: My wife cooks very well. She cooks... she makes borshch, you know borshch?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Yes, I know borshch! It's a beetroot soup, right? Ukrainian food is delicious!

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Yes! Best food! She make amazing borshch. I take to work in... how to say... контейнер.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: A container! Or you can say 'a lunch box'. 'I take it to work in a lunch box.' And remember - she makes, with the 's'.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: She makes amazing borshch. I take it to work in a lunch box.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: That's wonderful! Now, let me ask you about your morning. What time does your alarm go off?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Alarm? Ah, будильник. My alarm go off at quarter to seven. Six forty-five.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Good use of 'quarter to seven'! And remember - 'my alarm goes off'. It goes, with an 's'.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: My alarm goes off at quarter to seven. But I... how to say... I press button and sleep more. Five minutes.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Ha! You hit the snooze button! That's what we call it - 'snooze'. You press snooze and sleep for five more minutes. Many people do that!

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Snooze! Yes, snooze. I hit snooze button maybe three times.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: The snooze button - three times! So you actually get up at seven then?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Yes, about seven. Then I go to bathroom, I wash face, brush teeth.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Good vocabulary! 'I wash my face and brush my teeth.' We need 'my' before face and teeth. Can you say that?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: I wash my face and brush my teeth.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: And then what do you do?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Then I have breakfast. Usually I eat sandwich and drink coffee. I love coffee, I can not live without coffee!

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Ha! I'm the same! And we say 'I can't live without coffee' - 'can't', not 'can not'. Well, both are technically correct, but 'can't' is much more natural. What kind of coffee do you drink?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: I drink... strong coffee. Black. No milk, no sugar. Real coffee!

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Strong black coffee! I prefer mine with milk but strong coffee is very popular. And after breakfast?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: After breakfast I go to work. I go on car. Drive maybe... thirty minutes if traffic is okay.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: So here we say 'I go by car' or even better 'I drive to work'. We don't say 'on car' or 'go on car'. We use 'by' for transport - by car, by bus, by train.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: By car. I drive to work. Thirty minutes by car.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Exactly! And what about your wife? How does she get to work?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: She go by... marshrutka. You know marshrutka? Is like small bus.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Ah yes, a minibus! 'She goes by minibus' - or by marshrutka if you want to use the Ukrainian word. Remember - she goes, with the 's'!

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: She goes by marshrutka. By minibus. It take... takes? It takes twenty minutes.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Great self-correction! 'It takes twenty minutes.' Well done! You caught the 's' yourself!

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: I try to remember!

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: You're doing really well. Now let's practise some more daily routine vocabulary. I'm going to say some actions and I want you to tell me when you do them. Ready?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Yes, ready.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Okay. Wake up.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: I wake up at seven o'clock.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Have breakfast.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: I have breakfast at... quarter past seven? About seven fifteen.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Good! Leave home.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: I leave home at half past eight.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Start work.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: I start work at nine o'clock.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Have lunch.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: I have lunch at one o'clock.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Finish work.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: I finish work at six o'clock.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Come home.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: I come home at... half past six. Six thirty.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Have dinner.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: I have dinner at seven o'clock.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Go to bed.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: I go to bed at... eleven? Half past eleven. I sleep late.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: That is quite late! Especially if you wake up at seven. You need your sleep! How many hours do you sleep?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Maybe seven hours. Is not enough but... I watch YouTube in evening. About programming.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: 'In the evening' - with 'the'. And 'it's not enough'. Let me ask you something. After dinner, what do you do before bed? Tell me everything.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Okay. After dinner I... rest little bit. Maybe play with... ні, how to say... I play games on computer? No no, I mean... I spend time with wife. We watch TV or talk. Then I study English - this is why I am here! And then I watch YouTube about programming and go to bed.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: That's a full evening! Great description. A couple of small things - 'I rest a little bit' - we need 'a'. And 'I spend time with my wife'. Let's also add 'a' little bit, not just 'little bit'. Can you say those again?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: I rest a little bit. I spend time with my wife. These small words are... annoying!

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Ha! I know articles are tricky. But you're making progress! Now, let's put this all together and practise the present simple. Can you tell me about your whole day? Start from the morning.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Okay. I wake up at seven o'clock. I wash my face and brush my teeth. I have breakfast - sandwich and coffee. I drive to work at half past eight. I start work at nine. I have lunch at one o'clock in the office. I finish work at six. I drive home. I have dinner at seven. I rest a little bit, spend time with my wife, study English, watch YouTube, and go to bed at half past eleven.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Dmytro, that was brilliant! Really well structured. I'm impressed! Now, can you tell me about your wife's day? Remember what happens with the verbs...

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Oh, okay. My wife... she wake up at six o'clock.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: She...?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: She wakes up! Sorry. She wakes up at six o'clock.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Good! Keep going.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: She has breakfast. She goes to work by marshrutka. She... start work at eight.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: She starts...

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: She starts work at eight. She have lunch at... twelve?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: She...?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: She has lunch! At twelve. Then she finish... finishes work at three o'clock because school finish... finishes early.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Excellent self-correction! She finishes work at three, the school finishes early. Great! Then what does she do?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: She comes home and... cooks dinner. She cooks very well. She makes borshch, varenyky... many Ukrainian food.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: That sounds delicious! 'Many Ukrainian dishes' - we'd say 'dishes' rather than 'food' when we're listing specific things. Or 'lots of Ukrainian food'. Both work.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Many Ukrainian dishes. Okay.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: And does she have any hobbies in the evening?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: She read books. She likes... romance books? And she also watch TV. Korean dramas. She loves this.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: She reads books and watches TV. Remember those endings! And we'd say 'romance novels' or 'romantic books'. What time does she go to bed?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: She goes to bed at... ten o'clock. Before me. She says I am crazy because I sleep so late.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Ha! She says you're crazy because you go to bed so late. I think she might be right - seven hours isn't a lot of sleep! Okay, well done. You told me about your day and your wife's day really well.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Thank you!

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Now let's look at some important grammar. You already know the present simple a bit, but let's make sure the rules are clear. In present simple, for I, you, we, and they - the verb stays the same. I wake up, you wake up, we wake up. But for he, she, and it...?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: We add 's'. He wakes up.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Exactly! Now, some verbs are a little different. If the verb ends in 'sh', 'ch', 's', 'x', or 'o', we add 'es'. So 'watch' becomes 'watches', 'go' becomes 'goes', 'finish' becomes 'finishes'. Can you give me an example?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: She watches TV. He goes to work. She finishes at three.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Perfect! All three correct. Now, what about negatives? How do we make a negative in present simple?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: I... don't? I don't wake up early?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Yes! 'I don't wake up early.' We use 'don't' for I, you, we, they. And for he, she, it?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Doesn't? She doesn't?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Exactly! 'She doesn't wake up early.' And here's the important thing - when we use 'doesn't', the main verb goes back to the base form. Not 'she doesn't wakes', but 'she doesn't wake'. The 's' moves to 'does'. Make sense?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Hmm... so the 's' go... goes to 'does' and verb is normal? She doesn't wake up. Not she doesn't wakes up.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Exactly right! The 's' is in 'does', so the verb doesn't need it. Let's practise. Tell me something your wife doesn't do.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: My wife doesn't drink coffee. She drink... she drinks only tea.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: She doesn't drink coffee, she drinks only tea. Now tell me something you don't do.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: I don't cook. I am terrible in kitchen!

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Ha! 'I'm terrible in the kitchen!' - with 'the'. And we could also say 'at cooking'. 'I'm terrible at cooking.' Both work. So your wife does all the cooking?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Yes, all cooking. I... sometimes I try to make... як це сказати... яєчню... how to say... eggs?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Scrambled eggs? Or fried eggs? Яєчня is usually 'fried eggs'.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Yes! Fried eggs. I sometimes make fried eggs. This is my only... how to say... рецепт... recipe?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Recipe, yes! 'Fried eggs is my only recipe.' That's funny! At least you can make something. Okay, let me give you some more practice with negatives. I'm going to say a sentence and you make it negative. Ready?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Okay, ready.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: I eat meat.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: I don't eat meat.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: She likes fish.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: She doesn't like fish.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Good - not 'likes'! Just 'like'. He watches movies.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: He doesn't watch movies.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: We go to the gym.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: We don't go to the gym.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Excellent! You've got it. All perfect. Now, let me teach you something really useful for talking about routines - adverbs of frequency. These are words that tell us how often we do something.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Like always?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Exactly like 'always'! So we have a scale. At the top, one hundred percent of the time: always. Then usually - maybe eighty percent. Then often - maybe sixty-five percent. Sometimes - about fifty percent. Rarely or seldom - maybe ten percent. And never - zero percent.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Always, usually, often, sometimes, rarely, never. Okay. In Ukrainian similar - завжди, зазвичай, часто, іноді, рідко, ніколи.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Good parallel! Now, where do we put these words in a sentence? They go before the main verb. 'I always drink coffee.' 'She usually wakes up at six.' 'We sometimes go to a restaurant.' Can you give me an example?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: I always drink coffee in morning.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: 'In the morning' - with 'the'! Good sentence though. Another one?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: I never cook dinner.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Ha! That's true! What about with 'sometimes'?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Sometimes I go to restaurant with my wife.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Now that's interesting! You put 'sometimes' at the beginning. That's actually okay! 'Sometimes' and 'usually' can go at the beginning or before the verb. But 'always' and 'never' should go before the verb, not at the beginning. So 'Sometimes I go to a restaurant' is fine, but we wouldn't say 'Always I drink coffee'.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Hmm, so I cannot say 'Always I drink coffee'? Only 'I always drink coffee'?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Right! 'Always I drink coffee' sounds unnatural in English. Always goes before the verb. But 'sometimes' is flexible - it can go at the start or before the verb. Let me give you a quick quiz. Where does the adverb go?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: He - blank - eats breakfast. Always.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: He always eats breakfast.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: I - blank - take the bus. Usually.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: I usually take the bus.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: We - blank - eat out. Rarely.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: We rarely eat out. 'Eat out' means go to restaurant?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Exactly! 'Eat out' means to eat at a restaurant. Good question! Now tell me about your routine using these frequency words. For example, what do you always do?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: I always drink coffee in the morning. I always drive to work. I never cook.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Good! What about 'usually'?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: I usually wake up at seven. I usually have sandwich for breakfast.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: 'I usually have a sandwich' - with 'a'. What do you sometimes do?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: I sometimes eat out with my wife. I sometimes watch movie in evening.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: 'Watch a movie in the evening.' Two articles there! But great use of the frequency words. Now, can you tell me about your wife using these words?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: My wife always wake up at six.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: My wife always...?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Wakes! My wife always wakes up at six. She usually cook... cooks Ukrainian food. She sometimes reads books in the evening. She never drinks coffee.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Fantastic! That was really good, Dmytro. You caught most of the 's' endings and used the frequency adverbs correctly. One more tricky thing - with 'be', the adverb goes after the verb. 'She is always happy', not 'She always is happy'. But don't worry about that too much right now.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Oh, more rules! English have... has many rules.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: English has many rules - good self-correction! Yes, it does. But you're learning them fast. Let me now give you a little exercise. I want you to compare your workday with your weekend. What's different?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Okay. On workday... on work day, I wake up at seven. But on weekend I wake up at nine or ten. Very late.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: 'On a workday' and 'at the weekend' or 'on the weekend' - both are used. In British English we say 'at the weekend', in American English 'on the weekend'. Keep going!

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: At the weekend I don't go to work. I... stay at home. Or sometimes I go to... парк? Park.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Yes, park! You go to the park. What else?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: I sometimes go to supermarket. And I am sometimes meeting with friends. We go to pub or... café.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Good! A couple of corrections. 'I sometimes go to the supermarket' - with 'the'. And 'I sometimes meet friends', not 'I am sometimes meeting'. For regular habits, we use present simple, not present continuous. 'I meet friends', not 'I am meeting friends'.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: But in Ukrainian we can use both... I sometimes meet friends. Okay, present simple for habits.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Exactly! Present simple for habits and routines. Present continuous is for right now, this moment. 'I am talking to you right now.' But 'I talk to my teacher every Saturday' - that's a routine, so present simple.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: I talk to my teacher every Saturday. This is true! I am talking to you right now. And I talk to you every Saturday. Okay, I understand.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Brilliant example! You really understand the difference. Now let me ask you some questions. How often do you go to the gym?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: I never go to gym. I know, is bad. I am working in office all day and then I am sitting at home. No sport.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: So a few things here. 'I never go to the gym' - with 'the'. 'I work in the office all day and then I sit at home.' Remember - habits, routines? Present simple! Not 'I am working' and 'I am sitting', but 'I work' and 'I sit'.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Ah! I work in the office all day and I sit at home. Present simple because this is every day, habit.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Exactly! And 'it's bad', not 'is bad' - we need the subject 'it'. 'I know it's bad.' Good awareness though! Do you want to start going to the gym?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Always I want to start but... no, sorry. I always want to start but I never start. Maybe next week!

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Ha! You corrected yourself - 'I always want' not 'Always I want'. Well done! That's the word order we talked about. And yes, 'maybe next week' - that's what everyone says!

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: My wife tell me every day - go to gym! But I am lazy.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Your wife tells you every day! Remember that 's'. She tells you. Go on - how about cooking? How often do you cook?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: I never cook. Like I say before, I can make only fried eggs. My wife always cooks.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: 'Like I said before' - past tense there because you said it earlier. Good use of 'my wife always cooks' though! The 's' was there. Now, how often do you study English, apart from our lessons?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: I usually study English every day. Maybe thirty minutes. I use application... app on phone. And sometimes I watch English videos on YouTube.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: That's great! Every day is impressive. 'An app on my phone' - we'd need 'an' before 'app' and 'my' before 'phone'. How about reading in English?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: I sometimes read... news. Tech news. In English. But is difficult because I don't understand all words.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: 'It is difficult' - remember the subject 'it'. And 'I don't understand all the words.' That's completely normal though. You learn new words from context. Keep doing it!

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Yes, I try. Context helps.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Now, let's do something fun. Let's talk about an ideal day. If you had no work, no responsibilities - what would your perfect day look like? But let's keep it in present simple, like you're describing a routine. 'On my ideal day, I wake up at...'

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Okay! On my ideal day... I wake up at ten o'clock. No alarm! I have big breakfast - eggs, bread, coffee, maybe pancakes.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: 'I have a big breakfast' - with 'a'. Love the enthusiasm! Keep going.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: I have a big breakfast. Then I go to park... to the park with my wife. We walk and talk. Then we go to nice restaurant for lunch. I eat... стейк... steak!

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Steak for lunch - nice! 'We go to a nice restaurant.' Keep going!

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: After lunch I want to... hmm... I want to go to cinema. Watch new movie. Action movie maybe, or... science fiction.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: 'Go to the cinema' or 'go to a cinema'. And 'watch a new movie'. Those articles again! What happens after the cinema?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: After cinema... I want come home and play computer games. I like games but I don't have time usually. On ideal day I play maybe three, four hours!

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Ha! A gamer! So 'after the cinema I want to come home' - don't forget 'to' after 'want'. 'I want to come home and play computer games.' And 'on my ideal day' - with 'my'. Can you try that sentence again?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: After the cinema I want to come home and play computer games. On my ideal day I play maybe three or four hours.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Great! And then evening?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: In the evening my wife cooks special dinner. Or maybe we order food. Pizza or... sushi. Then we watch movie together and go to bed early. Maybe ten o'clock.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: 'Watch a movie together.' That sounds like a lovely ideal day! Very relaxing. Now, I noticed you used present simple really well there. Let me count - you said 'I wake up, I have, I go, we walk, we go, I eat, I want, I play, she cooks.' All correct! I'm very pleased.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Really? I think I am getting better!

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: You definitely are! Now let me check your understanding with some questions about your day. I want you to answer with full sentences using frequency adverbs. What time do you usually wake up on a workday?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: I usually wake up at seven o'clock on a workday.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: How do you usually get to work?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: I usually drive to work. It takes thirty minutes.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Excellent! Do you ever take the bus?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: No, I never take bus. I always drive.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: 'I never take the bus.' But great answer! Does your wife ever drive to work?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: No, she never drive to work. She doesn't have car. She always take... takes the marshrutka.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: 'She never drives to work.' 'She doesn't have a car.' Remember the 's' on 'drives' and the article 'a' before 'car'. You did catch 'takes' though - well done!

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: She never drives to work. She doesn't have a car. Okay.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Good. How often do you eat out?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: We rarely eat out. Maybe once in month. Because my wife cooks so well, we don't need restaurant.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: 'Once a month' - not 'in month'. And 'we don't need a restaurant' or 'we don't need to go to a restaurant'. Great sentence structure though!

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Once a month. We don't need a restaurant.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: What do you usually do after dinner?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: After dinner I usually rest a little bit. Then I sometimes study English. I always watch YouTube before bed.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: That was a perfect answer. Three frequency adverbs, all in the right position. Lovely! Now let's do a quick review of what we've learned today. Can you tell me the three ways to say time that we practised?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Okay. Half past - for thirty. Quarter past - for fifteen. And quarter to - for forty-five.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Brilliant! And what about past and to for other minutes?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Past is after hour, to is before next hour. Twenty past seven is seven twenty. Twenty to eight is seven forty.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: 'After the hour' and 'before the next hour' - but your explanation is spot on! Now, what are the daily routine verbs we used today? How many can you remember?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Wake up, wash face... wash my face, brush my teeth, have breakfast, drive to work, start work, have lunch, finish work, come home, have dinner, rest, study, watch TV, go to bed.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Wow, that's impressive! You remembered so many! Now, quick grammar check. What's the rule for present simple with he, she, and it?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Add 's' to verb. She wakes up, he goes to work, it takes thirty minutes.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: And for negatives?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: I don't for I, you, we, they. Doesn't for he, she, it. And verb go back to normal. She doesn't wake up early.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: 'The verb goes back to the base form.' But you explained it correctly! And frequency adverbs? Where do they go?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Before the verb. I always drink coffee. But sometimes can go at beginning of sentence too.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Excellent! You really understood today's lesson. Now, let me give you some practice sentences to check everything works together. I'll give you a situation and you make a sentence. Ready?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Ready!

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Your colleague - he - wake up - 6:00 - always.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: He always wakes up at six o'clock.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Your sister - she - eat breakfast - never.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: She never eats breakfast.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Perfect! You and your wife - go to cinema - sometimes.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: We sometimes go to the cinema.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: With the article! Well done! Your boss - not drink coffee - he.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: He doesn't drink coffee.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Your wife - cook dinner - usually - 7:00.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: My wife usually cooks dinner at seven o'clock.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: That is all perfect! Every single one correct. Dmytro, you should feel really good about this. Let me now give you something a bit more challenging. Can you describe a typical Monday for you, from morning to night, using at least five frequency adverbs?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Okay, I try. On Monday I always wake up at seven o'clock. I usually have coffee and sandwich for breakfast. I always drive to work. On Monday we usually have team meeting at ten o'clock. I sometimes have lunch with my colleague... colleagues. I always finish work at six but sometimes I finish late because of... how to say... дедлайн...

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Deadline! Same word - deadline.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Oh, same word! Deadline. So sometimes I finish late because of deadline. I usually come home at half past six. My wife always cooks dinner. We never eat out on Monday. After dinner I usually study English. I sometimes watch TV but not always because I am tired. I always go to bed before midnight on Monday.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Dmytro, that was amazing! I counted - always, usually, sometimes, never, always, usually, sometimes, always - eight frequency adverbs! And nearly all the grammar was correct. Just two small things - 'a coffee and a sandwich' - or just 'coffee and a sandwich'. And 'because of a deadline'. But honestly, that was really, really good.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Thank you! I feel like I am progress... I am making progress.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: You are making progress! Great self-correction there. Now, before we finish, I want to do one more activity. I'm going to ask you to tell me about someone else's routine - let's say your best friend. Tell me about his or her typical day.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Okay. My best friend is Oleksiy. He is programmer too. He is... he work... works from home because his company is in... Польща... Poland? Yes, Poland. He is remote.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: 'He works from home' - good correction! 'His company is in Poland' - perfect. 'He works remotely' or 'he's a remote worker'. Keep going, tell me about his day.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: He works remotely. He usually wakes up at eight o'clock because he don't... doesn't need to drive. He has breakfast at home. He starts work at nine. He always has many meetings because Poland company... the Polish company have many meetings.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: 'He doesn't need to drive' - good correction! 'The Polish company has many meetings' - remember 'has' for it/the company. Go on!

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: The Polish company has many meetings. He has lunch at home - he cook... he cooks for himself because he is not married. He is... як це... bachelor? He sometimes orders pizza because he is lazy to cook.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Bachelor - yes! 'He's too lazy to cook' - we need 'too' before 'lazy'. He's too lazy to cook. Great description though! What does he do in the evening?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: In the evening he usually play... plays computer games. He is big gamer. He sometimes plays until two or three in morning. He never goes to bed early!

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: 'He's a big gamer' - with 'a'. And 'two or three in the morning'. But Dmytro, I'm really impressed. You told me about Oleksiy's whole day and you caught most of the third person 's' yourself. That's real progress!

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: I try very hard to remember! Sometimes I forget but I am getting better I think.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: You absolutely are. The fact that you're self-correcting shows that you know the rule - it's just becoming automatic that takes time. It'll come with practice.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Thank you, this is very... how to say... мотивуюче... motivating?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Motivating - yes, perfect word! Now, let's talk about homework. I'd like you to do two things this week. First, write a short paragraph about your typical Saturday. Use present simple and at least four frequency adverbs. Can you do that?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Yes, I can do this. Write about Saturday with present simple and frequency adverbs.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Great! And second, I want you to practise telling the time. Every time you look at a clock this week, try to say the time in English using 'past', 'to', 'half past', and 'quarter'. Even just in your head.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Oh, this is good idea! Every time I see clock I say time in English. Quarter past nine, twenty to three...

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: 'Every time I see a clock' - with 'a'. Yes, exactly like that! It's a simple exercise but it really helps make it automatic.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Every time I see a clock. Okay, I will do. And the paragraph about Saturday.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: 'I will do it' - we need the object 'it'. Now, let me quickly review the main things to remember from today's lesson. Can you tell me the three most important things you learned?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: First, telling time - half past, quarter past, quarter to, and past and to for other minutes. Second, present simple with he, she, it - add 's'. She wakes up, he goes, it takes. Third, frequency adverbs - always, usually, sometimes, never - before the verb.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: That's a perfect summary! And what about the things you need to keep working on?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Articles! 'The', 'a', 'an'. I always forget. And the 's' for he and she. And prepositions - 'at' for time, not 'on'.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Excellent self-awareness! Articles are the number one challenge for Ukrainian speakers because Ukrainian doesn't have them. It takes time, but you're already getting better at catching yourself. The third person 's' is also very common - keep practising with your wife's routine, talking about what she does.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Yes, I can practice at home. I tell my wife - she cooks, she works, she reads. She will think I am strange!

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Ha! Tell her it's English homework! Actually, that's a great idea. When you describe what she's doing, use the third person. 'She's cooking dinner. She cooks dinner every day.' Real life practice is the best practice.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Okay, I will try this. She will laugh at me but is okay!

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: 'It's okay' - remember the 'it'. She'll laugh but it's okay. Now, before we finish, let me ask you a few quick revision questions. What time is it when I say 'quarter to three'?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Two forty-five.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Half past eleven?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Eleven thirty.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Twenty to six?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Five... forty.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Correct! Now make this sentence negative: 'He eats breakfast at home.'

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: He doesn't eat breakfast at home.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Where does 'usually' go in: 'They go to bed at ten.'?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: They usually go to bed at ten.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: All correct! Now one more - a tricky one. Make this sentence: your mother - always - not - forget - your birthday. Negative with a frequency adverb!

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Hmm... My mother never... no. My mother... doesn't always forget? No... My mother never forgets my birthday!

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Well, both could work! 'My mother never forgets my birthday' means she always remembers. 'My mother doesn't always forget' would mean she sometimes remembers, sometimes forgets. But I think 'never forgets' is what we want. And you got 'forgets' with the 's'. Brilliant!

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: This was confusing but I think I understand. Never forgets - because she always remembers.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Exactly right! Don't worry, that was a hard one. You did very well. So, to wrap up - how did you find today's lesson? Too easy, too hard, or just right?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: I think is... it is just right. Not too easy. The grammar about present simple I know a little bit before but the time and the frequency words... adverbs are new for me. And I need to practice more the 's' and articles.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: That's great feedback! 'I knew a little bit before' - past tense because you're talking about before the lesson. And yes, articles and the third person 's' will be ongoing. We'll keep practising those in every lesson.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Good. I like when we practice with real life examples. Like my routine and my wife routine. Is easier to remember.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: 'My wife's routine' - we need the possessive 's' here. And 'it's easier to remember'. I'm glad you find real examples helpful - I'll keep using them!

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: My wife's routine. Possessive 's'... different 's' again! English has too many 's'!

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Ha! That's a great observation! We have the plural 's' - two cats. The third person 's' - she walks. The possessive 's' - my wife's routine. And the contraction 's' - it's raining. Four different uses of 's'!

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Four! This is crazy. In Ukrainian we don't have this problem.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: True! But Ukrainian has cases, which are hard for English speakers to learn. Every language has its challenges!

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Yes, Ukrainian have... has seven cases. English people always say is impossible!

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: 'Ukrainian has seven cases' and 'English people always say it's impossible' - great content, just those small grammar points. Seven cases does sound scary! Alright Dmytro, we're almost at the end of our lesson. Let me give you a final summary.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Today we covered: telling the time with o'clock, half past, quarter past, quarter to, and minutes past and to. Daily routine vocabulary - wake up, have breakfast, drive to work, start work, have lunch, finish work, come home, have dinner, go to bed. Present simple for habits and routines, including the third person 's'. And adverbs of frequency - always, usually, often, sometimes, rarely, never.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Yes, very big lesson! A lot of information.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: It was a lot! But you handled it really well. Your homework: write a paragraph about your typical Saturday, and practise saying the time in English whenever you see a clock. And if you want bonus points, describe your wife's daily routine out loud - great practice for the third person 's'!

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: I will do all three! I am very motivated now. Thank you Sarah, this was great lesson.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: 'A great lesson' - with the article! You're proving my point that articles need work. But seriously, you did wonderfully today. Your present simple is getting much stronger and your vocabulary is growing fast.

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: A great lesson! Okay, I need to always remember articles. Thank you very much, Sarah!

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: 'I always need to remember' - the adverb goes before the main verb. But look at you, trying to use everything we learned! That's the right attitude. Same time next week?

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Yes, same time next week. At... quarter past ten on Saturday!

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Using the time vocabulary already! Excellent. Have a lovely week, Dmytro. Good luck with the bank project!

utterances.speaker: Dmytro
utterances.text: Thank you! Have a good week too. Bye bye!

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Bye, Dmytro! See you next Saturday.
